title: The inevitable weakness of metrics
gist: A personal essay and review of C. Thi Nguyen's book 'The Score' arguing that metrics are inherently limiting—not just misused—because quantification strips context to travel between settings; it introduces 'value capture' (outsourcing your values to external metrics) and warns that an AI era built on benchmarks guarantees machines will surpass humans by the metrics' own logic.

insights.claim: C. Thi Nguyen's 'value capture' describes adopting external metrics or rankings and letting them rule you without adapting them to your life, thereby outsourcing both your values and your sense of meaning.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for spotting where KPIs, ratings, or benchmarks silently redefine goals—applicable to institutions and markets, not just individuals.
insights.quote: In value capture, you're essentially outsourcing your values,
insights.locator: Over the years, people have invented

insights.claim: Value capture operates at every scale—restaurants chasing Yelp ratings, students chasing GPA, scientists chasing grants, pastors chasing baptism-rate leaderboards—displacing the original purpose with the proxy.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Shows how metric-driven incentive systems systematically corrupt institutional missions—a lens for evaluating organizations and management cultures.
insights.quote: It happens when students stop caring about education and start caring about their GPA.
insights.locator: Individuals, institutions, and indeed entire societies

insights.claim: Metrics inevitably redefine one's core sense of what's important whether or not one is aware of the trap.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Awareness alone does not immunize against metric capture—implying governance, not vigilance, is the only real defense.
insights.quote: Metrics inevitably redefine your core sense of what's important, whether you're aware of the trap or not.
insights.locator: Outside the health and fitness realm

insights.claim: Theodore Porter's insight is that quantification is a 'technology of distance' that minimizes the need for intimate knowledge and personal trust because metrics travel well between contexts and are easy to grasp and aggregate.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Explains why metrics dominate bureaucratic and market systems—portability and legibility, not accuracy, drive their adoption.
insights.quote: minimizes the need for intimate knowledge and personal trust.

insights.claim: To produce a metric legible across contexts you must strip out nuanced, qualitative information, so any institutional quantification is fundamentally limited in what it can measure—as with GPA or GDP.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: The legibility-vs-fidelity trade-off is structural, suggesting headline economic and performance metrics inherently omit what matters most.
insights.quote: To arrive at a clear metric, you inevitably need to simplify what you're attempting to measure, often jettisoning heaps of nuanced, qualitative, or open-ended information

insights.claim: GDP is driven ever higher by politicians because economists once concluded it correlates with economic well-being, an example of a simplified proxy becoming a societal target.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Frames headline macro metrics as proxies subject to value-capture distortion—useful skepticism for reading economic indicators.
insights.quote: the economic metric of GDP, which politicians and societies are now compelled to drive higher and higher

insights.claim: The author argues Goodhart's Law ('when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure') is commonly misread as user error, when the real point is that all measurements are targets and the failure is intrinsic to measurement itself.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Reframes a widely cited principle—if metric failure is structural rather than fixable by 'better metrics,' the standard remedy is futile.
insights.quote: All measurements, I would argue, are in fact targets, whether you intend them to be or not.

insights.claim: Real-world examples of measurement going wrong include pain quantified as the 'fifth vital sign' in the mid-1990s, which exacerbated the opioid epidemic, and the McNamara fallacy of deciding by what's easily measured.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Concrete cases where optimizing a metric caused systemic harm—evidence that metric misuse carries real downside, including in markets and policy.
insights.quote: the disastrous attempt to quantify pain as "the fifth vital sign" in the mid-1990s (which exacerbated the opioid epidemic)
insights.locator: Every so often I'll find myself

insights.claim: The author found that over 10-plus years of tracking steps, heart rate, sleep, calories, and engagement, he gained virtually no self-knowledge and felt worse, with goals escalating (6,000 steps to 20,000) as measurement begat more measurement.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: First-hand evidence that the self-tracking value proposition can be negative—relevant to wearables and health-tech adoption narratives.
insights.quote: the more I used numerical proxies, the worse I felt about pretty much everything.

insights.claim: The 'quantified self' movement, coined by Wired editors Gary Wolf and Kevin Kelly in 2007, was built on the premise that 'unless something can be measured, it cannot be improved.'
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Identifies the ideological root of the consumer self-tracking industry the author is critiquing.
insights.quote: Unless something can be measured, it cannot be improved,

insights.claim: The author argues that turning intelligence and creativity into benchmarks for AI to surpass guarantees defeat for humans, because in a world built around metrics machines will inevitably beat humans—that is what they are built to do.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: A pointed critique of benchmark-driven AI framing—suggests the human-vs-AI comparison is rigged by the choice to use metrics at all.
insights.quote: If we insist on expressing our worth through attentional metrics and productivity scores, if we continue to turn intelligence and creativity into a series of benchmarks for AI to surpass, we've already lost.

insights.claim: The author contends that believing numbers say something real about human needs gives them power, and that questioning that belief is the first step to pushing back against pervasive quantification.
insights.speaker: Bryan Gardiner
insights.so_what: Offers a practical counter-move to metric capture—skepticism of the underlying belief rather than seeking 'better' numbers.
insights.quote: believing that numbers say something real or useful about human needs and desires gives them power.
insights.locator: So how do we push back?
